question: Explain the four pillars of Object-Oriented Programming: Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, and Abstraction. Provide C++ examples for each.
mainConcepts: Encapsulation: bundling data and methods, hiding internal state
Inheritance: deriving new classes from existing ones (is-a)
Abstraction: hiding implementation details, showing only essentials
Polymorphism via virtual functions and function overloading
Inheritance types: single, multiple, multilevel, hierarchical
referenceAnswer: Object-Oriented Programming has four main pillars: (1) Encapsulation — wrapping data and functions into a single unit (class) and hiding internal details using access specifiers (private, protected). Example: class BankAccount { private: double balance; public: void deposit(double a) { balance += a; } }; — balance is hidden, only accessible through methods. (2) Inheritance — creating new classes from existing ones (is-a relationship). Example: class Student { ... }; class UndergraduateStudent : public Student { ... }; — UndergraduateStudent inherits all Student members. (3) Polymorphism — the ability to present the same interface for different data types. Compile-time: function overloading (same name, different parameters). Runtime: virtual functions where derived classes override base class methods. Example: class Shape { virtual void draw() = 0; }; class Circle : public Shape { void draw() override { ... } }; — calling draw() on a Shape pointer invokes the correct version. (4) Abstraction — showing only essential features and hiding implementation. Achieved through abstract classes (with pure virtual functions) and interfaces. Example: the Shape class above is abstract — you cannot instantiate it, but you can use it as a base for concrete shapes.

question: Explain the difference between compile-time and runtime polymorphism in C++. Provide code examples for function overloading, operator overloading, and virtual functions.
mainConcepts: Compile-time: resolved by compiler (early binding), faster
Function overloading: same name, different parameters
Operator overloading: redefine operator behavior for objects
Runtime: resolved at execution (late binding), via virtual functions
Virtual functions enable dynamic dispatch through base pointers
vtable (virtual table) stores function pointers for dispatch
Pure virtual functions make a class abstract
referenceAnswer: Polymorphism in C++ has two types: (1) Compile-time polymorphism (early binding/static polymorphism): resolved during compilation. Achieved through function overloading and operator overloading. Function overloading example: int add(int a, int b) { return a+b; } and double add(double a, double b) { return a+b; } — the compiler selects the correct version based on argument types. Operator overloading: returning Stream& operator<<(ostream& os, const Point& p) { os << '(' << p.x << ',' << p.y << ')'; return os; } allows cout << p;. (2) Runtime polymorphism (late binding/dynamic polymorphism): resolved during execution using virtual functions. Example: class Base { public: virtual void show() { cout << "Base"; } }; class Derived : public Base { void show() override { cout << "Derived"; } }; Base* b = new Derived(); b->show(); // prints "Derived". This uses a virtual table (vtable) — each class with virtual functions has a vtable storing function pointers, and each object has a vptr pointing to the vtable. Runtime polymorphism is key to the Template Method design pattern and enables writing generic code that works with many types through a common interface.
